seo.title: Roi Et Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Complete guide to Roi Et Province: Phra Maha Chedi Chai Mongkhon (tallest chedi in Isaan, 101m), Bueng Phalan Chai lake, Ku Phra Kona Khmer ruins, and silk weaving villages. Area: 8,299 km², 20 districts. Plan your Isaan visit.

overview: Roi Et — whose name translates literally as 'one hundred and one' — is a mid-sized Isaan province in the heart of northeastern Thailand, defined by a quiet, deeply Buddhist character and a proud cultural heritage that most international visitors never discover. The province is anchored by its namesake capital city, which wraps around the serene Bueng Phalan Chai lake: a large urban lake that doubles as a public park, recreation area, and evening social hub for locals. The city's skyline is dominated by the Phra Maha Chedi Chai Mongkhon, a 101-metre-tall golden chedi constructed in the 1990s and recognised as the tallest Buddhist tower in the Isaan region — a tangible symbol of the province's enduring devotion. Deeper into the countryside, the 11th-century Khmer sanctuary ruins at Ku Phra Kona link Roi Et to the great Angkor civilisation that once extended across the Khorat Plateau. The province sustains a distinguished silk weaving tradition — particularly the mudmee ikat silk associated with the local Phu Thai and Lao-origin communities — and hosts one of Isaan's most spectacular religious festivals: Bun Phawet, a multi-day Buddhist merit-making celebration built around the recitation of the complete Mahavessantara Jataka that draws tens of thousands of worshippers from across the region. Roi Et remains genuinely off the international tourist circuit, offering authentic Isaan culture, excellent regional food, and warm local hospitality at prices well below Thailand's more-visited destinations.
geography: Roi Et Province covers 8,299 km² on the flat to gently undulating Khorat Plateau in the upper central northeast, positioned in the Chi River basin. The terrain is predominantly low-lying agricultural land — vast expanses of rain-fed paddy fields, cassava plantations, and sugar cane — with few significant hills. The Chi River and its tributaries (notably the Mun River drainage network to the south) dissect the province, providing irrigation water for the region's intensive rice cultivation and creating numerous reservoirs and oxbow lakes across the landscape. Elevations range from roughly 140 to 200 metres above sea level. The provincial capital sits at the geographic heart of the province and is notable for the Bueng Phalan Chai, a natural lake of approximately 80 hectares that was expanded and landscaped in the mid-20th century. The climate follows the classic Isaan tropical savanna pattern: a brutal hot season from March to May (regularly exceeding 38°C), a monsoon wet season from June to October dominated by heavy afternoon thunderstorms, and a cool dry season from November to February when temperatures drop to 12–18°C at night — the most comfortable period for travel.
highlights: Phra Maha Chedi Chai Mongkhon — 101-metre-tall golden chedi, the tallest Buddhist tower in Isaan, housing sacred relics and offering panoramic views from upper terraces accessible by lift
Bueng Phalan Chai — large landscaped lake at the heart of Roi Et city, ringed by a walking path, cycle track, pedal boats, floating restaurants, and the city's most-visited evening market
Ku Phra Kona — Khmer-period laterite sanctuary ruins dating to the 11th century, one of the most significant Angkor-era sites in Roi Et and a connection to the civilisation that built Angkor Wat
Bun Phawet Festival — Roi Et's grandest annual Buddhist festival, featuring a ceremonial recitation of the complete Mahavessantara Jataka that draws tens of thousands of pilgrims from across Isaan
Mudmee silk weaving villages — hand-woven ikat silk in intricate geometric patterns produced by Phu Thai and ethnic Lao communities in villages throughout the province
Wat Buraphaphiram — major temple complex in the capital containing the seated Luang Pho Phra Song Mueang, a revered bronze Buddha image attracting merit-makers year-round
Roi Et National Museum — provincial museum displaying Dvaravati-period artefacts, Khmer bronzes, and ethnographic collections including traditional Isaan textiles and musical instruments
Seri Thai Monument — memorial at Wat Klang Mueang commemorating the Seri Thai resistance movement, an important historical site reflecting the province's role in World War II

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool dry season with temperatures of 12–28°C. Ideal for walking Bueng Phalan Chai lake, visiting the Chedi and Ku Phra Kona, and cycling through silk weaving villages. The Bun Phawet Festival typically falls in this period (exact date varies by lunar calendar — often February or March).
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Oct & Mar: October's end of the monsoon brings lush green rice paddies and full reservoirs at their most scenic, with sharply reduced rainfall. March is warm but still manageable before the brutal April–May heat peak. The Rocket Festival (Bun Bang Fai) in May marks the transition to wet season with spectacular fireworks and processions across Roi Et.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Apr–Sep: April and May are intensely hot (38–40°C). The monsoon season (June–September) brings daily heavy rain, but the countryside turns intensely green and lush. The Chedi's air-conditioned interior hall and the National Museum offer cool-season refuges. Hotel prices drop significantly and the lake and temples are uncrowded.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the most comfortable weather. Time a visit to coincide with Bun Phawet if the lunar calendar permits — this is one of the most immersive Buddhist cultural experiences in all of Isaan and rarely witnessed by foreign travellers. Check with the TAT Roi Et office for the exact annual date, which shifts each year.

topAttractions.name: Phra Maha Chedi Chai Mongkhon
topAttractions.description: The defining landmark of Roi Et Province and the tallest Buddhist chedi in the entire Isaan region at 101 metres. Constructed in the 1990s and completed in 1996, the gleaming gold-sheathed tower enshrines sacred Buddha relics brought from Sri Lanka and stands on the grounds of Wat Nong Waeng Luang on the western edge of the city. The chedi's design blends traditional Thai and Isaan Buddhist architectural forms, with nine levels each symbolising a Buddhist aspiration. An internal lift carries visitors up through the chedi's core, with each floor displaying religious artworks, sacred objects, and panoramic views over the Roi Et plains from upper terraces. The surrounding temple grounds contain a large reclining Buddha image, ornate gates, and manicured gardens. The tower is illuminated after dark and is visible from across the city.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Bueng Phalan Chai
topAttractions.description: The heart of Roi Et city: a large natural lake of approximately 80 hectares that was expanded and landscaped into the province's premier public space. The lake is encircled by a well-maintained 8-kilometre promenade used daily by walkers, joggers, and cyclists. Pedal boats are available for hire on the water. The eastern shore hosts a cluster of floating restaurants where locals dine on grilled fish, som tam, and Isaan barbecue while looking out over the water. An evening market sets up on the southern lakeside with food stalls, fresh produce, and local crafts. At the centre of the lake, connected by a causeway, stands the Roi Et City Pillar shrine and a small island garden. The lake's morning light — with the Chedi visible on the horizon — is a favourite of local photographers.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free. Pedal boats: approximately 40–60 THB per 30 minutes

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Ku Phra Kona
topAttractions.description: A significant Khmer-period laterite and sandstone sanctuary ruin located in Suang district, approximately 50 km north of the provincial capital. Dating to the late 11th or early 12th century CE and constructed in the Baphuon architectural style associated with the Khmer Empire under King Udayadityavarman II, Ku Phra Kona is the most architecturally substantial Angkor-era monument in Roi Et Province. The central prang (tower sanctuary) stands on a raised laterite platform surrounded by partially collapsed subsidiary structures, enclosure walls, and the remnants of a ceremonial approach. The site's lintel carvings — depicting scenes from Hindu mythology including Vishnu reclining on the cosmic ocean — are of notable artistic quality. The site is managed by the Fine Arts Department and has an open-air interpretive shelter with bilingual signage.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Roi Et National Museum
topAttractions.description: The provincial museum housed in a modern building near the city centre, displaying four centuries of material culture from Roi Et and the surrounding Isaan region. The permanent collection is organised chronologically: prehistoric pottery and bronze tools from the Khorat Plateau's pre-historic settlements; Dvaravati-period Buddhist sculptures and boundary stones (bai sema); Khmer bronzes and sandstone architectural fragments recovered from sites including Ku Phra Kona; and ethnographic displays covering traditional Isaan life — silk looms, agricultural tools, musical instruments, ceremonial dress, and festival objects. The textile gallery is particularly strong, showcasing the full range of Roi Et's mudmee silk weaving traditions with looms, dye samples, and finished textiles from different district traditions.

sources.sourceName: Fine Arts Department Thailand — National Museums
Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Mudmee Silk Weaving Villages
topAttractions.description: Roi Et is one of Thailand's most respected centres for mudmee ikat silk — hand-woven fabric produced through a resist-dyeing technique where individual threads are tied and dyed in complex patterns before being woven on traditional floor looms, creating the characteristic blurred-edge geometric designs. The weaving tradition is strongest in the villages of Ban Sida (Suwannaphum district) and surrounding communities, where Phu Thai and ethnic Lao families have woven silk for generations. The OTOP (One Tambon One Product) programme has elevated Roi Et mudmee to national recognition: the province's silk is sold in Bangkok's finest craft emporiums. Visiting the villages — watching dye baths, thread preparation, and the rhythmic work of the loom — is one of the most genuinely immersive cultural experiences Roi Et offers.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to visit villages. Silk products from 200 THB (scarves) to several thousand THB (full cloth lengths)

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Wat Buraphaphiram
topAttractions.description: One of Roi Et city's most important and most visited temple complexes, located close to Bueng Phalan Chai lake. The temple is the home of Luang Pho Phra Song Mueang, a large and deeply revered bronze seated Buddha image in the Laotian-influenced Isaan style, widely regarded as the spiritual protector of the province. Merit-makers arrive throughout the day to offer flowers, incense, and gold leaf. The bot (ordination hall) features elaborate painted murals depicting scenes from the Buddha's life rendered in a vivid Isaan folk-art style. The temple grounds also contain a tall white stupa, a large reclining Buddha pavilion, and well-tended gardens popular with local residents in the early morning.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free. Dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered)

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

topAttractions.name: Bung Phra Wihan (Phi Man Reservoir)
topAttractions.description: A large reservoir in the north of the province that serves as both an irrigation resource and a popular ecotourism destination, particularly during the cool season when migratory waterbirds roost and feed on the shallow margins. Open-billed storks, purple herons, painted storks, and numerous duck species have been recorded here during the November–February migration period, making it one of Roi Et's quietly rewarding birdwatching locations. The surrounding landscape of flooded fields and low wooded hills is typical of the northern Roi Et countryside. Local fishermen work the reservoir year-round, and freshwater fish dishes — particularly grilled pla chon (snakehead fish) — are available at simple restaurants near the water.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Roi Et

localFood: Som Tam Pla Ra — Isaan-style green papaya salad with fermented mudfish (pla ra), raw long beans, cherry tomatoes, and a heavy hit of dried chilies; the daily staple eaten at every meal in Roi Et households
Khao Niao — steamed glutinous sticky rice in woven bamboo kratip baskets, the inescapable Isaan accompaniment eaten by hand alongside grilled meats, lap, and fermented fish dishes

Gai Yang Roi Et — charcoal-grilled whole chicken marinated in a paste of lemongrass, galangal, garlic, coriander root, and fish sauce; a roadside speciality eaten with sticky rice and sweet-spicy jeow dipping sauce

Lap Wua — minced beef salad with toasted rice powder, lime juice, fish sauce, dried chili flakes, shallots, and fresh mint and sawtooth coriander; the beef version of Roi Et's celebrated lap, reflecting the province's cattle-raising tradition

Tom Saep — a spicy, sour, aromatic Isaan soup built on pork ribs or offal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, dried chilies, and pla ra; bolder and more intensely flavoured than Central Thai tom yum

Sai Krok Isan — fermented pork-and-rice sausage grilled over charcoal until the skin is crackling-crisp; eaten with raw cabbage wedges, fresh ginger slices, and whole green chilies at morning and evening markets
Pla Chon Tod — deep-fried whole snakehead fish (pla chon) served with a sharp green mango and fermented fish dipping sauce; a speciality of the floating restaurants on Roi Et's reservoirs

Khao Tom Mud — sweet sticky rice cooked with coconut milk and black beans, folded into banana leaves and grilled until the leaf chars; sold at temple markets and festival stalls throughout the province
culture: Roi Et's cultural identity is rooted in deeply conservative Theravada Buddhism and the Lao-influenced folk traditions common across the upper Chi River basin. The province's most defining cultural moment is Bun Phawet — the 'festival of the Mahavessantara Jataka' — one of the grandest Buddhist merit-making celebrations in the Isaan calendar. Held over several days in the cool or early hot season (date varies by lunar calendar), Bun Phawet involves the ceremonial recitation of the complete Mahavessantara Jataka (the story of the Buddha's penultimate life as Prince Vessantara) by monks working in relay, accompanied by elaborate processions, temple decorations, food offerings, and theatrical performances. It draws pilgrims from across Isaan and is considered one of the highest acts of Buddhist merit available to a lay person. The silk weaving tradition — particularly the mudmee ikat style produced in villages like Ban Sida and throughout Suwannaphum district — is a source of profound local pride, with OTOP certification and royal patronage elevating Roi Et textiles to national prominence. Mor lam music thrives here: the province has produced nationally known mor lam singers and still hosts tent-stage performances at every temple fair, wedding, and village festival. The name 'Roi Et' itself — 'one hundred and one' — is said to derive from the province's original network of 101 ponds or muang (principalities), a piece of local lore that reinforces the province's deep attachment to its own history.
gettingThere: Roi Et has a domestic airport (ROI) served by Nok Air with direct flights from Bangkok Suvarnabhumi and Don Mueang (approximately 1 hour). From Bangkok by road, air-conditioned buses depart from Mo Chit Northern Bus Terminal operated by Nakhonchai Air and regional carriers, covering the approximately 520 km journey in 7–8 hours (350–500 THB). Overnight buses are the most time-efficient option from Bangkok. By rail, there is no direct rail service to Roi Et; the most practical rail approach is to Ubon Ratchathani or Khon Kaen and transfer by bus. From Khon Kaen (120 km west), frequent minivans and air-con buses cover the journey in approximately 2 hours (80–120 THB). From Kalasin (100 km north), minivans depart regularly. From Ubon Ratchathani (120 km southeast), buses connect via Yasothon in approximately 2.5 hours.
gettingAround: Roi Et city centre is walkable between Bueng Phalan Chai lake, the main temples, and the night market area. Local songthaews run fixed routes within Mueang Roi Et for 10–20 THB per trip. Tuk-tuks and motorcycle taxis operate within the city for 40–80 THB. For sites outside the capital — Ku Phra Kona (50 km north in Suang district) and the silk weaving villages (30–60 km from the capital) — private transport is effectively essential. Motorbike rental in Roi Et city is available for 200–300 THB per day from shops near the market and bus terminal, and is the most flexible option for independent exploration. Hiring a private car with driver for a full-day circuit costs 1,000–1,500 THB. Public minivans connect Mueang Roi Et to major district towns (Suwannaphum, Selaphum, Suang) for 30–60 THB from the bus terminal.
travelTips: The Phra Maha Chedi Chai Mongkhon is most photogenic at golden hour before sunset — the 101m tower catches the light dramatically; arrive by 4:30 PM and combine with a walk along Bueng Phalan Chai lake as the evening market sets up

Ku Phra Kona is rarely visited by foreign travellers — you may have the entire Khmer ruins to yourself; visit in the cool morning (7–10 AM) when light is ideal for photography and before the midday heat

The silk weaving villages are best visited on weekdays when weavers are actively working — weekends are quieter. Ban Sida in Suwannaphum district is the most accessible; ask your accommodation for current opening information
Bun Phawet dates shift annually with the lunar calendar — confirm the exact dates with TAT Roi Et (tel: 043-511-272) before planning around the festival

Bueng Phalan Chai evening market (south shore, from 5 PM) offers excellent value Isaan food — a full meal of gai yang, som tam, and sticky rice costs 80–120 THB; arrive early before the most popular dishes sell out
Roi Et is a practical hub for an Isaan circuit: Kalasin is 100 km north, Yasothon is 70 km east, and Maha Sarakham is 75 km west — plan a loop rather than an out-and-back trip
Few restaurants in Roi Et outside the capital have English menus — photograph dishes at neighbouring tables or use a translation app; most food stalls respond well to pointing
The Roi Et National Museum provides essential historical context for Ku Phra Kona's carvings and the silk tradition — visit it before heading into the countryside
